definition: Collected facts, measurements, or observations used for analysis. Data can be numerical (quantitative) or categorical (qualitative).
subject: basic-statistics
examples: A student survey recording ages, heights, and degree subjects produces data. Ages and heights are numerical; degree subjects are categorical.

definition: A subset of individuals selected from a larger population to represent it. We study the sample because studying every member of the population is usually impractical.
subject: basic-statistics
examples: To study the study habits of 5,000 students, a researcher randomly selects 200 of them. Those 200 students form the sample.

definition: The entire group of individuals or observations that a study is interested in. In practice, we almost never measure the whole population.
subject: basic-statistics
examples: If a researcher wants to know the average height of all first-year university students in the UK, the population is every first-year UK university student.

definition: The arithmetic average of a set of values, found by adding all values and dividing by how many there are. It is sensitive to extreme values (outliers).
subject: basic-statistics

definition: The middle value when data is sorted in order. If there is an even number of values, the median is the mean of the two middle values. The median is resistant to outliers.
subject: basic-statistics

definition: The value (or values) that appears most frequently in a dataset. A dataset can have no mode, one mode, or multiple modes.
subject: basic-statistics
examples: In the dataset \(2, 3, 3, 4, 5, 5, 5, 6\), the mode is \(5\) because it appears three times, more than any other value.

definition: A measure of how spread out the values in a dataset are from the mean. It is the average of the squared differences from the mean.
subject: basic-statistics

definition: The square root of the variance. It measures spread in the same units as the original data, making it easier to interpret than variance.
subject: basic-statistics
examples: If exam scores have a mean of \(65\) and standard deviation of \(10\), most students scored between \(55\) and \(75\).

definition: The difference between the largest and smallest values in a dataset. A simple but crude measure of spread that is sensitive to outliers.
subject: basic-statistics

definition: Values that divide a sorted dataset into four equal parts. The first quartile (Q1) is the 25th percentile, the second (Q2) is the median, and the third (Q3) is the 75th percentile.
subject: basic-statistics

definition: The difference between the third and first quartiles. It measures the spread of the middle 50% of the data and is resistant to outliers.
subject: basic-statistics
examples: If Q1 \(= 20\) and Q3 \(= 35\), then IQR \(= Q_3 - Q_1 = 15\). Values more than \(1.5 \times 15 = 22.5\) beyond Q1 or Q3 are flagged as outliers.

definition: A number between \(0\) and \(1\) that describes how likely an event is to occur. A probability of \(0\) means impossible; \(1\) means certain.
subject: basic-statistics
examples: The probability of rolling a \(6\) on a fair die is \(P(6) = \frac{1}{6} \approx 0.167\).

definition: A description of all possible outcomes of a random variable and how likely each is. For discrete variables it's a table or formula; for continuous variables it's a curve.
subject: basic-statistics
examples: The probability distribution for one fair die lists each outcome (1 through 6) with probability \(\frac{1}{6}\) each, summing to \(1\).

definition: A variable whose value is the numerical outcome of a random process. It can be discrete (countable outcomes) or continuous (any value in a range).
subject: basic-statistics
examples: Let \(X\) be the number of heads in \(3\) coin flips. \(X\) is a discrete random variable taking values \(0, 1, 2, 3\).

definition: The long-run average outcome of a random variable if you repeated the experiment many times. Calculated by multiplying each outcome by its probability and summing.
subject: basic-statistics
examples: For a fair die: \(E(X) = 1\cdot\frac{1}{6} + 2\cdot\frac{1}{6} + \cdots + 6\cdot\frac{1}{6} = \frac{21}{6} = 3.5\).

definition: A symmetric, bell-shaped probability distribution that describes many natural phenomena. It is completely defined by its mean and standard deviation.
subject: basic-statistics
examples: Heights of adults tend to follow a normal distribution. If the mean is \(170\) cm and standard deviation \(8\) cm, about \(68\%\) of people are between \(162\) and \(178\) cm.

definition: The number of standard deviations a data point is from the mean. It standardises values so you can compare across different scales.
subject: basic-statistics
examples: A student scores \(80\) in an exam with mean \(65\) and SD \(10\). Their z-score is \(z = \frac{80-65}{10} = 1.5\), meaning they scored 1.5 standard deviations above average.

definition: A fundamental theorem stating that the distribution of sample means approaches a normal distribution as sample size increases, regardless of the shape of the original population distribution.
subject: basic-statistics
examples: Even if individual exam scores are skewed, the distribution of average scores across many random samples of \(30\) students will look approximately normal.

definition: A formal statistical procedure used to decide whether observed data provide enough evidence to reject a specific claim (the null hypothesis).
subject: basic-statistics
examples: A researcher claims a coin is fair. They flip it 100 times and get 65 heads. A hypothesis test checks whether this result is surprising enough to reject the 'fair coin' claim.

definition: The default assumption that there is no effect or no difference. A hypothesis test tries to find evidence against it.
subject: basic-statistics
examples: In a drug trial, the null hypothesis is: 'The new drug has no effect on recovery time.' We reject this only if the data strongly suggest otherwise.

definition: The hypothesis that contradicts the null hypothesis — the claim you are trying to find evidence for.
subject: basic-statistics
examples: If \(H_0\) says the drug has no effect, then the alternative hypothesis \(H_1\) might say: 'The drug reduces recovery time.'

definition: The probability of observing results at least as extreme as the data, assuming the null hypothesis is true. A small p-value suggests the data are unusual under \(H_0\).
subject: basic-statistics
examples: A p-value of \(0.03\) means: if the null hypothesis were true, there is only a \(3\%\) chance of seeing data this extreme. This is typically considered strong evidence against \(H_0\).

definition: A threshold probability (usually \(0.05\) or \(5\%\)) set before conducting a test. If the p-value falls below this threshold, the result is called statistically significant.
subject: basic-statistics
examples: With significance level \(\alpha = 0.05\): if the p-value is \(0.02\), we reject \(H_0\). If the p-value is \(0.08\), we do not reject \(H_0\).

definition: Rejecting the null hypothesis when it is actually true — a false positive. The probability of a Type I error equals the significance level \(\alpha\).
subject: basic-statistics
examples: A medical test incorrectly concludes a healthy patient has the disease. This is a Type I error.

definition: Failing to reject the null hypothesis when it is actually false — a false negative.
subject: basic-statistics
examples: A drug that actually works fails to show a statistically significant effect in a small study. Concluding the drug has no effect is a Type II error.

definition: A measure of how strongly and in what direction two variables are linearly related. It ranges from \(-1\) (perfect negative) to \(+1\) (perfect positive), with \(0\) meaning no linear relationship.
subject: basic-statistics
examples: Height and weight are positively correlated (\(r \approx 0.7\)): taller people tend to weigh more, but the relationship is not perfect.

definition: A method for finding the best-fit line (or curve) through data to model the relationship between a response variable and one or more predictor variables.
subject: basic-statistics
examples: Simple linear regression finds the line \(\hat{y} = a + bx\) that minimises the total squared distances from each data point to the line.

definition: The probability distribution of the number of successes in a fixed number of independent trials, each with the same probability of success.
subject: basic-statistics
examples: Tossing a fair coin \(10\) times: \(X \sim B(10, 0.5)\). The probability of exactly \(6\) heads is \(P(X=6) = \binom{10}{6}(0.5)^{10} \approx 0.205\).

definition: A range of values calculated from sample data that is likely to contain the true population parameter. A 95% confidence interval means that if you repeated the study many times, 95% of such intervals would contain the true value.
subject: basic-statistics
examples: A 95% CI for a mean exam score: \(72 \pm 4\), giving the interval \([68, 76]\). We are 95% confident the true population mean lies in this range.

definition: A data point that lies far from the rest of the dataset. Outliers can result from measurement error or indicate genuinely unusual cases. They can distort the mean and standard deviation.
subject: basic-statistics
examples: In the dataset \(10, 12, 11, 13, 85\), the value \(85\) is an outlier. The mean rises to \(26.2\) because of it, while the median (\(12\)) is unaffected.

definition: A systematic error introduced when the sample is not representative of the population, causing conclusions to be skewed in a predictable direction.
subject: basic-statistics
examples: Surveying only students in the library to measure average study hours will over-represent students who study a lot, introducing sampling bias.

definition: The standard deviation of the sampling distribution of a statistic (usually the mean). It measures how much the sample mean is expected to vary from sample to sample.
subject: basic-statistics
examples: If population SD \(= 20\) and sample size \(= 100\), then \(SE = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} = \frac{20}{10} = 2\). Larger samples give smaller standard errors.
